html:
XII

Ten days later Adam bought some flowers at the corner of Wigmore Street and went to call on Miss Runcible at her nursing home. He was shown first into the matronвАЩs room. She had numerous photographs in silver frames and a very nasty fox terrier. She smoked a cigarette in a greedy way, making slight sucking noises.

вАЬJust taking a moment off in my den,вАЭ she explained. вАЬDown, Spot, down. But I can see youвАЩre fond of dogs,вАЭ she added, as Adam gave Spot a halfhearted pat on the head. вАЬSo you want to see Miss Runcible? Well, I ought to warn you first that she must have no kind of excitement whatever. SheвАЩs had a severe shock. Are you a relation, may I ask?вАЭ

вАЬNo, only a friend.вАЭ

вАЬA very special friend, perhaps, eh?вАЭ said the Matron archly. вАЬNever mind, IвАЩll spare your blushes. Just you run up and see her. But not more than five minutes, mind, or youвАЩll have me on your tracks.вАЭ

There was a reek of ether on the stairs which reminded Adam of the times when, waiting to take her to luncheon, he had sat on NinaвАЩs bed while she did her face. (She invariably made him turn his back until it was over, having a keen sense of modesty about this one part of her toilet, in curious contrast to some girls, who would die rather than be seen in their underclothes, and yet openly flaunt unpainted faces in front of anyone.)

It hurt Adam deeply to think much about Nina.

Outside Miss RuncibleвАЩs door hung a very interesting chart which showed the fluctuations of her temperature and pulse and many other curious details of her progress. He studied this with pleasure until a nurse, carrying a tray of highly polished surgical instruments, gave him such a look that he felt obliged to turn away.

Miss Runcible lay in a high, narrow bed in a darkened room.

A nurse was crocheting at her side when Adam entered. She rose, dropping a few odds and ends from her lap, and said, вАЬThereвАЩs someone come to see you, dear. Now remember you arenвАЩt to talk much,вАЭ She took the flowers from AdamвАЩs hand, said, вАЬLook, what lovelies. ArenвАЩt you a lucky girl?вАЭ and left the room with them. She returned a moment later carrying them in a jug of water. вАЬThere, the thirsties,вАЭ she said. вАЬDonвАЩt they love to get back to the nice cool water?вАЭ

Then she went out again.

вАЬDarling,вАЭ said a faint voice from the bed, вАЬI canвАЩt really see who it is. Would it be awful to draw the curtains?вАЭ

Adam crossed the room and let in the light of the grey December afternoon.

вАЬMy dear, how blind-making. There are some cocktail things in the wardrobe. Do make a big one. The nurses love them so. ItвАЩs such a nice nursing home this, Adam, only all the nurses are starved, and thereвАЩs a breathtaking young man next door who keeps putting his head in and asking how I am. He fell out of an aeroplane, which is rather grand, donвАЩt you think?вАЭ

вАЬHow are you feeling, Agatha?вАЭ

вАЬWell, rather odd, to tell you the truth.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HowвАЩs Nina?вАЭ

вАЬSheвАЩs got engaged to be marriedвБ†вАФhavenвАЩt you heard?вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, the nurses are interested in no one but Princess Elizabeth. Do tell me.вАЭ

вАЬA young man called Ginger.вАЭ

вАЬWell?вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt you remember him? He came on with us after the airship party.вАЭ

вАЬNot the one who was sick?вАЭ

вАЬNo, the other.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt rememberвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ does Nina call him Ginger?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬWhy?вАЭ

вАЬHe asked her to.вАЭ

вАЬWell?вАЭ

вАЬShe used to play with him when they were children. So sheвАЩs going to marry him.вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, isnвАЩt that rather sad-making for you?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm desperate about it. IвАЩm thinking of committing suicide, like Simon.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt do that, darlingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ did Simon commit suicide?вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, you know he did. The night all those libel actions started.вАЭ

вАЬOh, that Simon. I thought you meant Simon.вАЭ

вАЬWhoвАЩs Simon?вАЭ

вАЬThe young man who fell out of the aeroplane. The nurses call him Simple Simon because itвАЩs affected his brainsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but, Adam I am sorry about Nina. IвАЩll tell you what weвАЩll do. As soon as IвАЩm well again weвАЩll make Mary Mouse give a lovely party to cheer you up.вАЭ

вАЬHavenвАЩt you heard about Mary?вАЭ

вАЬNo, what?вАЭ

вАЬShe went off to Monte Carlo with the Maharajah of Pukkapore.вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, arenвАЩt the Mice furious?вАЭ

вАЬSheвАЩs just receiving religious instruction before her official reception as a royal concubine. Then theyвАЩre going to India.вАЭ

вАЬHow people are disappearing, Adam. Did you get that money from the drunk Major?вАЭ

вАЬNo, he disappeared too.вАЭ

вАЬDвАЩyou know, all that time when I was dotty I had the most awful dreams. I thought we were all driving round and round in a motor race and none of us could stop, and there was an enormous audience composed entirely of gossip writers and gatecrashers and Archie Schwert and people like that, all shouting to us at once to go faster, and car after car kept crashing until I was left all alone driving and drivingвБ†вАФand then I used to crash and wake up.вАЭ

Then the door opened, and Miles came popping in.

вАЬAgatha, Adam, my dears. The time IвАЩve had trying to get in. I canвАЩt tell you how bogus they were downstairs. First I said I was Lord Chasm, and that wasnвАЩt any good; and I said I was one of the doctors, and that wasnвАЩt any good; and I said I was your young man, and that wasnвАЩt any good; and I said I was a gossip writer, and they let me up at once and said I wasnвАЩt to excite you, but would I put a piece in my paper about their nursing home. How are you, Aggie darling? I brought up some new records.вАЭ

вАЬYou are angelic. Do letвАЩs try them. ThereвАЩs a gramophone under the bed.вАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs a whole lot more people coming to see you today. I saw them all at luncheon at MargotвАЩs. Johnny Hoop and Van and Archie Schwert. I wonder if theyвАЩll all manage to get in.вАЭ

They got in.

So soon there was quite a party, and Simon appeared from next door in a very gay dressing-gown, and they played the new records and Miss Runcible moved her bandaged limbs under the bedclothes in negro rhythm.

Last of all, Nina came in looking quite lovely and very ill.

вАЬNina, I hear youвАЩre engaged.вАЭ

вАЬYes, itвАЩs very lucky. My papa has just put all his money into a cinema film and lost it all.вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, it doesnвАЩt matter at all. My papa lost all his twice. It doesnвАЩt make a bit of difference. ThatвАЩs just one of the things one has to learn about losing all oneвАЩs money.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Is it true that you really call him Ginger?вАЭ

вАЬWell, yes, only, Agatha, please donвАЩt be unkind about it.вАЭ

And the gramophone was playing the song which the black man sang at the Caf√© de la Paix.

Then the nurse came in.

вАЬWell, you are noisy ones, and no mistake,вАЭ she said. вАЬI donвАЩt know what the matron would say if she were here.вАЭ

вАЬHave a chocolate, sister?вАЭ

вАЬOoh, chocs!вАЭ

Adam made another cocktail.

Miles sat on Miss RuncibleвАЩs bed and took up the telephone and began dictating some paragraphs about the nursing home.

вАЬWhat it is to have a friend in the Press,вАЭ said the nurse.

Adam brought her a cocktail. вАЬShall I?вАЭ she said. вАЬI hope you havenвАЩt made it too strong. Suppose it goes to my head? What would the patients think if their sister came in tiddly. Well, if youвАЩre sure it wonвАЩt hurt me, thanks.вАЭ

вАЬвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yesterday I visited the Hon.¬†Agatha Runcible comma Lord ChasmвАЩs lovely daughter comma at the Wimpole Street nursing home where she is recovering from the effects of the motor accident recently described in this column stop Miss Runcible was entertaining quite a large party which includedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Adam, handing round cocktails, came to Nina.

вАЬI thought we were never going to meet each other again.вАЭ

вАЬWe were obviously bound to, werenвАЩt we?вАЭ

вАЬAgathaвАЩs looking better than I expected, isnвАЩt she? What an amusing nursing home.вАЭ

вАЬNina, I must see you again. Come back to LottieвАЩs this evening and have dinner with me.вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬPlease.вАЭ

вАЬNo. Ginger wouldnвАЩt like it.вАЭ

вАЬNina, you arenвАЩt in love with him?вАЭ

вАЬNo, I donвАЩt think so.вАЭ

вАЬAre you in love with me?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I was once.вАЭ

вАЬNina, IвАЩm absolutely miserable not seeing you. Do come and dine with me tonight. What can be the harm in that?вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, I know exactly what it will mean.вАЭ

вАЬWell, why not?вАЭ

вАЬYou see, GingerвАЩs not like us really about that sort of thing. HeвАЩd be furious.вАЭ

вАЬWell, what about me? Surely I have first claim?вАЭ

вАЬDarling, donвАЩt bully. Besides, I used to play with Ginger as a child. His hair was a very pretty colour then.вАЭ

вАЬвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Mr.¬†вАШJohnnyвАЩ Hoop, whose memoirs are to be published next month, told me that he intends to devote his time to painting in future, and is going to Paris to study in the spring. He is to be taken into the studio ofвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬFor the last time, NinaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬWell, I suppose I must.вАЭ

вАЬAngel!вАЭ

вАЬI believe you knew I was going to.вАЭ

вАЬвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Miss Nina Blount, whose engagement to Mr.¬†вАШGingerвАЩ Littlejohn, the well-known polo player.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Mr.¬†SchwertвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬIf only you were as rich as Ginger, Adam, or only half as rich. Or if only you had any money at all.вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ said the Matron, appearing suddenly. вАЬWhoever heard of cocktails and a gramophone in a concussion case? Sister Briggs, pull down those curtains at once. Out you go, the whole lot of you. Why, IвАЩve known cases die with less.вАЭ

Indeed, Miss Runcible was already showing signs of strain. She was sitting bolt upright in bed, smiling deliriously, and bowing her bandaged head to imaginary visitors.

вАЬDarling,вАЭ she said. вАЬHow too divineвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ how are you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and how are you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ how angelic of you all to comeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ only you must be careful not to fall out at the cornersвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ooh, just missed it. There goes that nasty Italian carвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I wish I knew which thing was which in this carвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ darling, do try and drive more straight, my sweet, you were nearly into me then.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ FasterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs all right, Miss Runcible, thatвАЩs all right. You mustnвАЩt get excited,вАЭ said the Matron. вАЬSister Briggs, run for the ice-pack quickly.вАЭ

вАЬAll friends here,вАЭ said Miss Runcible, smiling radiantly. вАЬFaster.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ FasterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ itвАЩll stop all right when the time comesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

That evening Miss RuncibleвАЩs temperature went rocketing up the chart in a way which aroused great interest throughout the nursing home. Sister Briggs, over her evening cup of cocoa, said she would be sorry to lose that case. Such a nice bright girlвБ†вАФbut terribly excitable.

At ShepheardвАЩs Hotel Lottie said to Adam:

вАЬThat chapвАЩs been in here again after you.вАЭ

вАЬWhat chap, Lottie?вАЭ

вАЬHow do I know what chap? Same chap as before.вАЭ

вАЬYou never told me about a chap.вАЭ

вАЬDidnвАЩt I dear? Well, I meant to.вАЭ

вАЬWhat did he want?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt knowвБ†вАФsomething about money. Dun, I expect. Says he is coming back tomorrow.вАЭ

вАЬWell, tell him IвАЩve gone to Manchester.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs right, dear.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What about a glass of wine?вАЭ

Later that evening Nina said: вАЬYou donвАЩt seem to be enjoying yourself very much tonight.вАЭ

вАЬSorry, am I being a bore?вАЭ

вАЬI think I shall go home.вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬAdam, darling, whatвАЩs the matter?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Nina, do you ever feel that things simply canвАЩt go on much longer?вАЭ

вАЬWhat dвАЩyou mean by thingsвБ†вАФus or everything?вАЭ

вАЬEverything.вАЭ

вАЬNoвБ†вАФI wish I did.вАЭ

вАЬI dare say youвАЩre rightвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ what are you looking for?вАЭ

вАЬClothes.вАЭ

вАЬWhy?вАЭ

вАЬOh, Adam, what do you wantвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ youвАЩre too impossible this evening.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt letвАЩs talk any more, Nina, dвАЩyou mind?вАЭ

Later he said: вАЬIвАЩd give anything in the world for something different.вАЭ

вАЬDifferent from me or different from everything?вАЭ

вАЬDifferent from everythingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ only IвАЩve got nothingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ whatвАЩs the good of talking?вАЭ

вАЬOh, Adam, my dearestвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYes?вАЭ

вАЬNothing.вАЭ

When Adam came down next morning Lottie was having her morning glass of champagne in the parlour.

вАЬSo your little birdвАЩs flown, has she? Sit down and have a glass of wine. That dunвАЩs been in again. I told him you was in Manchester.вАЭ

вАЬSplendid.вАЭ

вАЬSeemed rather shirty about it. Said heвАЩd go and look for you.вАЭ

вАЬBetter still.вАЭ

Then something happened which Adam had been dreading or days. Lottie suddenly said:

вАЬAnd that reminds me. What about my little bill?вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes,вАЭ said Adam, вАЬIвАЩve been meaning to ask for it. Have it made out and sent up to me some time, will you?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve got it here. Bless you, what a lot you seem to have drunk.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I do, donвАЩt I? Are you sure some of this champagne wasnвАЩt the JudgeвАЩs?вАЭ

вАЬWell, it may have been,вАЭ admitted Lottie. вАЬWe get a bit muddled with the books now and then.вАЭ

вАЬWell, thank you so much, IвАЩll send you down a cheque for this.вАЭ

вАЬNo, dear,вАЭ said Lottie. вАЬSuppose you write it down here. HereвАЩs the pen, hereвАЩs the ink, and hereвАЩs a blank cheque book.вАЭ

(Bills are delivered infrequently and irregularly at LottieвАЩs, but when they come, there is no getting away from them.) Adam wrote out a cheque for seventy-eight pounds sixteen shillings.

вАЬAnd twopence for the cheque,вАЭ said Lottie.

And twopence, Adam added.

вАЬThereвАЩs a dear,вАЭ said Lottie, blotting the cheque and locking it away in a drawer. вАЬWhy, look whoвАЩs turned up. If it isnвАЩt Mr.¬†Thingummy.вАЭ

It was Ginger.

вАЬGood morning, Mrs.¬†Crump,вАЭ he said rather stiffly.

вАЬCome and sit down and have a glass of wine, dear. Why I knew you before you were born.вАЭ

вАЬHullo, Ginger,вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬLook here, Symes,вАЭ said Ginger, looking in an embarrassed manner at the glass of champagne which had been put into his hand, вАЬI want to speak to you. Perhaps we can go somewhere where we shanвАЩt be disturbed.вАЭ

вАЬBless you, boys, I wonвАЩt disturb you,вАЭ said Lottie. вАЬJust you have a nice talk. IвАЩve got lots to see to.вАЭ

She left the parlour, and soon her voice could be heard raised in anger against the Italian waiter.

вАЬWell?вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬLook here, Symes,вАЭ said Ginger, вАЬwhat I mean to say is, what IвАЩm going to say may sound damned unpleasant, you know, and all that, but look here, you know, damn it, I mean the better man wonвБ†вАФnot that I mean IвАЩm the better man. WouldnвАЩt say that for a minute. And, anyway, NinaвАЩs a damn sight too good for either of us. ItвАЩs just that IвАЩve been lucky. Awful rough luck on you, I mean, and all that, but still, when you come to think of it, after all, well, look here, damn it, I mean, dвАЩyou see what I mean?вАЭ

вАЬNot quite,вАЭ said Adam gently. вАЬNow tell me again. Is it something about Nina?вАЭ

вАЬYes, it is,вАЭ said Ginger in a rush. вАЬNina and I are engaged, and IвАЩm not going to have you butting in or thereвАЩll be hell to pay.вАЭ He paused, rather taken aback at his own eloquence.

вАЬWhat makes you think IвАЩm butting in?вАЭ

вАЬWell, hang it all, she dined with you last night, didnвАЩt she, and stayed out jolly late, too.вАЭ

вАЬHow do you know how late she stayed out?вАЭ

вАЬWell, as a matter of fact, you see I wanted to speak to her about something rather important, so I rang her up once or twice and didnвАЩt get an answer until three oвАЩclock.вАЭ

вАЬI suppose you rang her up about every ten minutes?вАЭ

вАЬOh no, damn it, not as often as that,вАЭ said Ginger. вАЬNo, no, not as often as that. I know it sounds rather unsporting and all that, but you see I wanted to speak to her, and, anyway, when I did get through, she just said she had a pain and didnвАЩt want to talk: well, I mean to say. After all, I mean, one is a gentleman. It isnвАЩt as though you were just a sort of friend of the family, is it? I mean, you were more or less engaged to her yourself, werenвАЩt you, at one time? Well, what would you have thought if IвАЩd come butting in? You must look at it like that, from my point of view, too, mustnвАЩt you, I mean?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I think thatвАЩs rather what did happen.вАЭ

вАЬOh no, look here, Symes, I mean, damn it; you mustnвАЩt say things like that. DвАЩyou know all the time I was out East I had NinaвАЩs photograph over my bed, honest I did. I expect you think thatвАЩs sentimental and all that, but what I mean is I didnвАЩt stop thinking of that girl once all the time I was away. Mind you, there were lots of other frightfully jolly girls out there, and I donвАЩt say I didnвАЩt sometimes get jolly pally with them, you know, tennis and gymkhana and all that sort of thing, I mean, and dancing in the evenings, but never anything serious, you know. Nina was the only girl I really thought of, and IвАЩd sort of made up my mind when I came home to look her up, and if sheвАЩd have meвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ see what I mean? So you see itвАЩs awfully rough luck on me when someone comes butting in. You must see that, donвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬAnd thereвАЩs another thing, you know, sentiment and all that apart. I mean NinaвАЩs a girl who likes nice clothes and things, you know, comfort and all that. Well, I mean to say, of course, her fatherвАЩs a topping old boy, absolutely one of the best, but heвАЩs rather an ass about money, if you know what I mean. What I mean, NinaвАЩs going to be frightfully hard up, and all that, and I mean you havenвАЩt got an awful lot of money, have you?вАЭ

вАЬI havenвАЩt any at all.вАЭ

вАЬNo, I mean, thatвАЩs what I mean. Awfully rough on you. No one thinks the worse of you, respects you for it, I mean earning a living and all that. Heaps of fellows havenвАЩt any money nowadays. I could give you the names of dozens of stout fellows, absolute toppers, who simply havenвАЩt a bean. No, all I mean is, when it comes to marrying, then that does make a difference, doesnвАЩt it?вАЭ

вАЬWhat youвАЩve been trying to say all this time is that youвАЩre not sure of Nina?вАЭ

вАЬOh, rot, my dear fellow, absolute bilge. Damn it, IвАЩd trust Nina anywhere, of course I would. After all, damn it, what does being in love mean if you canвАЩt trust a person?вАЭ

(вАЬWhat, indeed?вАЭ thought Adam), and he said, вАЬNow, Ginger, tell the truth. WhatвАЩs Nina worth to you?вАЭ

вАЬGood Lord, why what an extraordinary thing to ask; everything in the world of course. IвАЩd go through fire and water for that girl.вАЭ

вАЬWell, IвАЩll sell her to you.вАЭ

вАЬNo, why, look here, good God, damn it, I meanвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll sell my share in her for a hundred pounds.вАЭ

вАЬYou pretend to be fond of Nina and you talk about her like that! Why, hang it, itвАЩs not decent. Besides, a hundred pounds is the deuce of a lot. I mean, getting married is a damned expensive business, donвАЩt you know. And IвАЩm just getting a couple of polo ponies over from Ireland. ThatвАЩs going to cost a hell of a lot, what with one thing and another.вАЭ

вАЬA hundred down, and I leave Nina to you, I think itвАЩs cheap.вАЭ

вАЬFifty.вАЭ

вАЬA hundred.вАЭ

вАЬSeventy-five.вАЭ

вАЬA hundred.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm damned if IвАЩll pay more than seventy-five.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll take seventy-eight pounds sixteen and twopence. I canвАЩt go lower than that.вАЭ

вАЬAll right, IвАЩll pay that. You really will go away?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll try, Ginger. Have a drink.вАЭ

вАЬNo, thank youвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ this only shows what an escape NinaвАЩs hadвБ†вАФpoor little girl.вАЭ

вАЬGoodbye, Ginger.вАЭ

вАЬGoodbye, Symes.вАЭ

вАЬYoung Thingummy going?вАЭ said Lottie, appearing in the door. вАЬI was just thinking about a little drink.вАЭ

Adam went to the telephone-box.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬHullo, is that Nina?вАЭ

вАЬWhoвАЩs speaking, please? I donвАЩt think Miss Blount is in.вАЭ

вАЬMr.¬†Fenwick-Symes.вАЭ

вАЬOh, Adam. I was afraid it was Ginger. I woke up feeling I just couldnвАЩt bear him. He rang up last night just as I got in.вАЭ

вАЬI know. Nina, darling, something awfulвАЩs happened.вАЭ

вАЬWhat?вАЭ

вАЬLottie presented me with her bill.вАЭ

вАЬDarling, what did you do?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I did something rather extraordinary.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ My dear, I sold you.вАЭ

вАЬDarlingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ who to?вАЭ

вАЬGinger. You fetched seventy-eight pounds sixteen and twopence.вАЭ

вАЬWell?вАЭ

вАЬAnd now I never am going to see you again.вАЭ

вАЬOh, but Adam, I think this is beastly of you; I donвАЩt want not to see you again.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm sorry.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Goodbye, Nina, darling.вАЭ

вАЬGoodbye, Adam, my sweet. But I think youвАЩre rather a cad.вАЭ

Next day Lottie said to Adam, вАЬYou know that chap I said came here asking for you?вАЭ

вАЬThe dun?вАЭ

вАЬWell, he wasnвАЩt a dun. IвАЩve just remembered. HeвАЩs a chap who used to come here quite a lot until he had a fight with a Canadian. He was here the night that silly Flossie killed herself on the chandelier.вАЭ

вАЬNot the drunk Major?вАЭ

вАЬHe wasnвАЩt drunk yesterday. Not so as youвАЩd notice anyway. Red-faced chap with an eyeglass. You ought to remember him, dear. He was the one made that bet for you on the November Handicap.вАЭ

вАЬBut I must get hold of him at once. WhatвАЩs his name?вАЭ

вАЬAh, that I couldnвАЩt tell you. I did know, but itвАЩs slipped my memory. HeвАЩs gone to Manchester to look for you. Pity your missing him!вАЭ

Then Adam rang up Nina. вАЬListen,вАЭ he said. вАЬDonвАЩt do anything sudden about Ginger. I may be able to buy you back. The drunk Major has turned up again.вАЭ

вАЬBut, darling, itвАЩs too late. Ginger and I got married this morning. IвАЩm just packing for our honeymoon. WeвАЩre going in an aeroplane.вАЭ

вАЬGinger wasnвАЩt taking any chances, was he? Darling, donвАЩt go.вАЭ

вАЬNo, I must. Ginger says he knows a вАШtophole little spot not far from Monte with a very decent nine-hole golf course.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬWell?вАЭ

вАЬYes, I knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ we shall only be away a few days. WeвАЩre coming back to spend Christmas with papa. Perhaps we shall be able to arrange something when we get back. I do hope so.вАЭ

вАЬGoodbye.вАЭ

вАЬGoodbye.вАЭ

Ginger looked out of the aeroplane: вАЬI say, Nina,вАЭ he shouted, вАЬwhen you were young did you ever have to learn a thing out of a poetry book about: вАШThis scepterвАЩd isle, this earth of majesty, this something or other EdenвАЩ? DвАЩyou know what I mean? вАШthis happy breed of men, this little world, this precious stone set in the silver seaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАШThis blessed plot, this earth, this realm, this England
This nurse, this teeming womb of royal kings
Feared by their breed and famous by their birthвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩ

I forget how it goes on. Something about a stubborn Jew. But you know the thing I mean?вАЭ

вАЬIt comes in a play.вАЭ

вАЬNo, a blue poetry book.вАЭ

вАЬI acted in it.вАЭ

вАЬWell, they may have put it into a play since. It was in a blue poetry book when I learned it. Anyway, you know what I mean?вАЭ

вАЬYes, why?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I mean to say, donвАЩt you feel somehow, up in the air like this and looking down and seeing everything underneath. I mean, donвАЩt you have a sort of feeling rather like that, if you see what I mean?вАЭ

Nina looked down and saw inclined at an odd angle a horizon of straggling red suburb; arterial roads dotted with little cars; factories, some of them working, others empty and decaying; a disused canal; some distant hills sown with bungalows; wireless masts and overhead power cables; men and women were indiscernible except as tiny spots; they were marrying and shopping and making money and having children. The scene lurched and tilted again as the aeroplane struck a current of air.

вАЬI think IвАЩm going to be sick,вАЭ said Nina.

вАЬPoor little girl,вАЭ said Ginger. вАЬThatвАЩs what the paper bags are for.вАЭ

There was rarely more than a quarter of a mile of the black road to be seen at one time. It unrolled like a length of cinema film. At the edges was confusion; a fog spinning past: вАЬFaster, faster,вАЭ they shouted above the roar of the engine. The road rose suddenly and the white car soared up the sharp ascent without slackening speed. At the summit of the hill there was a corner. Two cars had crept up, one on each side, and were closing in. вАЬFaster,вАЭ cried Miss Runcible, вАЬFaster.вАЭ

вАЬQuietly, dear, quietly. YouвАЩre disturbing everyone. You must lie quiet or youвАЩll never get well. EverythingвАЩs quite all right. ThereвАЩs nothing to worry about. Nothing at all.вАЭ

They were trying to make her lie down. How could one drive properly lying down?

Another frightful corner. The car leant over on two wheels, tugging outwards; it was drawn across the road until it was within a few inches of the bank. One ought to brake down at the corners, but one couldnвАЩt see them coming lying flat on oneвАЩs back like this. The back wheels wouldnвАЩt hold the road at this speed. Skidding all over the place.

вАЬFaster. Faster.вАЭ

The stab of a hypodermic needle.

вАЬThereвАЩs nothing to worry about, dearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ nothing at allвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ nothing.вАЭ
